questions.prompt: Why does free-text model output break production systems even when it 'usually' looks right?
options: Programs consume the output, and per-field failure rates compound across fields and volume
Free text is rejected by HTTP APIs
Models refuse to produce plain text in production environments
Free text can't be stored in databases
questions.explanation: A human forgives a preamble or a drifting key name; a parser doesn't. If each field is 98% reliable, a ten-field extraction fails roughly one time in five — and at production volume 'usually fine' means broken constantly.

questions.prompt: Why doesn't temperature 0 solve output format drift?
options: Temperature 0 disables JSON output entirely
The failure is structural: chat tuning rewards narration and sampling enforces no syntax, so determinism just repeats the same failure shapes
Temperature 0 is not supported by most providers
It does solve it — format drift only occurs above temperature 0.7
questions.explanation: Format failures aren't randomness you can sample away. Models are tuned to be conversational (preambles, explanations) and nothing in token sampling enforces grammar, so even greedy decoding produces fences, trailing commas, and drifting keys on some inputs.

questions.prompt: The lesson distinguishes three levels of output guarantee. Which are they? (Select all that apply.)
options: The output parses as JSON
The output matches your schema
The values in the output are actually correct
The output was generated at temperature 0
questions.explanation: Parses → matches schema → values correct is the guarantee ladder. Tooling (JSON mode, constrained decoding, validators) can deliver the first two; only evaluation (Module 7) can measure the third. Temperature is a sampling setting, not a guarantee.

questions.prompt: What role should regex-based JSON extraction play in a production pipeline?
options: It's the recommended primary parsing strategy
A salvage layer at most — the real strategy is schema-first: define Pydantic/Zod schemas and validate every response
It replaces the need for schema validation
It should run before the model call to pre-validate the prompt
questions.explanation: Stripping code fences or grabbing the first {...} block can rescue borderline outputs, but building on regex means building on sand. Define the schema first, validate every single response against it, and treat extraction tricks as a last-resort salvage layer.

questions.prompt: What does constrained decoding do that JSON mode does not?
options: It guarantees the output values are factually correct
It masks invalid tokens at decode time so output conforms to your specific schema, not just valid JSON syntax
It makes generation free of charge
It removes the need to define a schema
questions.explanation: JSON mode guarantees *some* syntactically valid JSON; constrained decoding (strict schemas) restricts sampling at each step to tokens that keep the output conformant with *your* JSON Schema. Neither guarantees the values are semantically correct — that's still on your evals.

questions.prompt: What is the portable way to get schema-conformant output from Anthropic's API?
options: A response_format parameter identical to OpenAI's
Forced tool use — define a tool whose input schema is your output schema and force the model to call it
Setting temperature to 0 with a JSON example
Anthropic models cannot produce structured output
questions.explanation: With Anthropic, you define a tool whose input schema is the shape you want and force the model to 'call' it via tool_choice — the tool arguments are your structured output. It's the same schema-as-output-channel idea as OpenAI's Structured Outputs, expressed through tool use.

questions.prompt: Why should you define your schema once in Pydantic or Zod and derive the JSON Schema from it?
options: Hand-written JSON Schema is rejected by the APIs
Maintaining the schema and the validator separately guarantees they eventually disagree
Pydantic and Zod schemas use fewer tokens
Providers only accept schemas generated by these libraries
questions.explanation: One source of truth serves both purposes: it generates the JSON Schema sent to the API and validates the response that comes back. Two hand-maintained copies will drift, and the bug will surface as validation failures on outputs that matched the (stale) request schema.

questions.prompt: Even with 'guaranteed' structured output enabled, which failure modes survive? (Select all that apply.)
options: Model refusals arriving instead of the schema
Truncation when the output hits max_tokens
Semantically wrong values in syntactically perfect fields
Output that is not valid JSON at all
questions.explanation: Strict schemas deliver syntax and shape, so invalid JSON is essentially eliminated — but a refusal can replace the object, max_tokens can cut it off mid-structure, and nothing stops a perfectly-shaped field from holding a wrong value. Validate every response and keep evals (Module 7) on values.

questions.prompt: Which latency metric does streaming primarily improve, and why?
options: Total generation time, because streaming makes the model decode faster
Perceived latency: users see output at time-to-first-token instead of waiting for the full response
Network latency, because SSE compresses tokens
Prefill time, because the prompt is processed incrementally
questions.explanation: Streaming doesn't speed up the model — decoding takes the same time. It exploits token-by-token generation so users see output after TTFT (network + prefill) rather than after the entire response. Tokens/second then determines how long the full answer takes.

questions.prompt: What transport do LLM streaming APIs use?
options: WebSockets with a binary protocol
Server-Sent Events — a long-lived HTTP response emitting 'data:' events
gRPC bidirectional streams
Repeated short polling requests
questions.explanation: Streaming responses are SSE: one HTTP response that stays open and emits data: events until completion. SDKs wrap this as async iterators so you rarely touch the wire format, but knowing it's SSE explains proxy buffering issues and reconnection behavior.

questions.prompt: Which statements about streaming mechanics are true? (Select all that apply.)
options: Usage (token counts) and stop_reason arrive at the end of the stream
Abandoning a stream early can break your cost logging
Partial JSON from a stream can be safely parsed with a standard parser at any point
Mid-stream disconnects require an explicit policy for handling partial output
questions.explanation: Final metadata arrives in the closing events, so dropping a stream early loses usage data; disconnects leave you holding partial output you must decide to retry, salvage, or discard. Partial JSON does NOT parse with a standard parser — that's the core streaming/structure tension.

questions.prompt: How should partial parses of a streaming JSON response be used?
options: For rendering progressive UI only — side effects wait for the complete, schema-validated final output
For database writes, as long as each partial parse succeeds
They shouldn't exist; streaming must be disabled for JSON outputs
As the final output once the stream is 90% complete
questions.explanation: Tolerant partial parsing (or SDK typed partial streams) is great for showing fields as they arrive. But a field can change or prove truncated until the stream ends, so anything irreversible — writes, side effects — must act only on the complete, validated final object.

questions.prompt: In the tool-calling flow, who actually executes the function?
options: The model executes it inside the provider's infrastructure
Your code: the model only emits the function name and arguments; you execute and return a tool_result
The provider runs it in a sandbox and returns the value
The function executes automatically when the schema is registered
questions.explanation: The model never runs anything. It emits a structured request — name plus schema-conformant JSON arguments — and stops. Your code executes the call (or refuses to) and sends the result back as a tool_result message, after which the model continues.

questions.prompt: A tool call fails because the database is down. What should you return to the model?
options: Nothing — silently retry until it succeeds
An exception that terminates the conversation
The error as a tool_result, so the model can adapt or report the problem
A fabricated success response to keep the conversation flowing
questions.explanation: Returning execution errors as tool results lets the model recover: retry with different arguments, fall back to another tool, or tell the user what failed. Swallowing or fabricating results produces confidently wrong behavior downstream.

questions.prompt: Which are tool-schema design best practices? (Select all that apply.)
options: Specific names and descriptions that say when the tool should be used
Per-parameter descriptions and enums for constrained values
Exposing one generic 'execute(command: string)' tool to maximize flexibility
Evaluating tool selection behavior like any other model behavior
questions.explanation: Tool schemas are prompts: precise names, when-to-use descriptions, documented parameters, and enums all steer the model's choices, and those choices deserve evals (Module 7). One generic command-string tool maximizes blast radius and minimizes the model's ability to choose well.

questions.prompt: What are parallel tool calls?
options: Running the same tool twice to verify the result
The model emitting several independent tool calls in one turn, which you can execute concurrently and answer in a single message
Two models calling tools against each other
A provider feature that shards one tool call across servers
questions.explanation: When operations don't depend on each other (fetch weather for three cities), the model can request them all in one turn. You execute them concurrently and return all tool_results together — fewer round trips, lower latency. This loop is the skeleton Module 5 grows into agents.

questions.prompt: How are images represented to a multimodal model?
options: As a URL the model browses at generation time
As image tokens in the same context window as text — consuming budget and participating in attention
As a separate channel that doesn't affect context limits
As OCR-extracted text only
questions.explanation: Images are encoded into token sequences that sit alongside your text in the context window. They cost money per request (roughly width×height/750 tokens on Anthropic's API — about 1,600 for a 1092×1092 image), count against the window, and are attended to like any other input.

questions.prompt: Why can native PDF input beat the classic 'extract text, send text' pipeline?
options: It's always cheaper in tokens
Providers give the model rendered page images plus text, preserving layout meaning that extraction destroys — tables, columns, forms
Text extraction is rate-limited by providers
Native input skips the context window entirely
questions.explanation: Multi-column layouts, tables whose meaning lives in geometry, and forms all lose information when flattened to a text stream. Native document input lets the model see the rendered page. The trade-off is token cost — hence hybrid pipelines that send images only for layout-heavy pages.

questions.prompt: Which practices improve multimodal requests? (Select all that apply.)
options: Placing the image before the text that asks about it
Cropping to the region of interest instead of sending a full-resolution screenshot
Labeling multiple images explicitly in your text
Upscaling small images so the provider won't downscale them
questions.explanation: Image-before-question measurably helps, cropping keeps small text readable after provider downscaling, and explicit labels make multi-image references unambiguous. Upscaling adds no information — it just spends more tokens on the same pixels.

questions.prompt: Why must values extracted from document images be validated like untrusted user input?
options: Because providers inject watermarks into extracted values
Because vision models confidently misread blurry text and invent cells — and documents can even carry prompt-injection instructions
Because image tokens are sampled at higher temperature
Because schemas don't apply to multimodal outputs
questions.explanation: Vision hallucination is real: blurry digits get misread and missing table cells get invented, all delivered with confidence. Validate ranges, cross-check totals, and route low-confidence docs to humans. And since document content enters the context, it can contain injected instructions — Module 8 covers those defenses.

questions.prompt: Rank the three structured-output guarantees from weakest to strongest assurance about correctness.
options: Parses as JSON → matches your schema → values actually correct
Matches schema → parses → correct
Correct → parses → matches schema
They're equivalent guarantees
questions.explanation: JSON mode gets you parsing, constrained decoding gets you schema conformance, and only evals measure value correctness — tooling can never deliver the third level.

questions.prompt: You're streaming a JSON response to a UI and writing the result to a database. What's the correct split?
options: Write to the DB as soon as the first field parses
Render partial parses progressively; write to the DB only from the complete, schema-validated final output
Disable streaming — it's incompatible with JSON
Buffer everything, defeating streaming, in all cases
questions.explanation: Partial parses are for rendering only; fields can change or prove truncated until the stream ends. Side effects wait for validated final output.

questions.prompt: In the tool-calling loop, the model 'calls' a tool. What literally happens?
options: The provider executes the function server-side
The model emits a name + schema-conformant arguments and stops; your code executes and returns a tool_result
The function runs inside the model's weights
The API queues a webhook
questions.explanation: The model only requests; your code disposes. That separation is where validation, approval gates, and error-as-result recovery all live — and it's the skeleton of Module 5's agents.

questions.prompt: Which design choices reuse one schema definition across the whole pipeline? (Select all that apply.)
options: Define once in Pydantic/Zod and derive the JSON Schema for the API
Use the same schema to validate every response
Hand-write a separate JSON Schema for the request and keep it in sync manually
Reuse the production schema as an eval check (Module 7)
questions.explanation: One source of truth serves request schema, response validation, and eval checks. Separately maintained copies will drift and disagree.

questions.prompt: Your invoice-extraction feature sends a photo and gets schema-perfect JSON back. Why validate the values anyway?
options: Schemas expire after 30 days
Vision models confidently misread blurry digits and invent cells — schema conformance says nothing about value correctness
JSON from images is a different dialect
Validation makes the response cheaper
questions.explanation: Multimodal + structured output is the killer combination, but the guarantee ladder still applies: cross-check totals, validate ranges, route low confidence to humans.

questions.prompt: Which latency metric does streaming improve, and which technique from this module compounds it for repeated large prompts?
options: TTFT perceived latency; prompt caching (Module 8) cuts the prefill behind it
Tokens/second; bigger GPUs
Total duration; retries
Network latency; compression
questions.explanation: Streaming shows output at time-to-first-token; caching the stable prompt prefix shrinks prefill, which is most of TTFT for big prompts.
